description: Other key distributions for risk and quant work — Lognormal, Poisson and Student's t — with use cases, limitations and short exercises. (The Normal distribution has its own dedicated topic.)
primer:
## What this set covers
A one-page recap of the four distributions you'll be tested on, and when each applies. Skip it if you already know this — the cards test your understanding (theory, intuition and a few exercises), and every card's answer is here or one short step from it.

## Normal (Gaussian)
- Symmetric bell, fixed entirely by mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$; skewness 0, no excess kurtosis; supported on the **whole real line** $(-\infty,\infty)$.
- The **68–95–99.7 rule**; z-scores standardise to $N(0,1)$ via $z=(x-\mu)/\sigma$; key one-sided quantiles **1.645 / 1.96 / 2.33** (95% / 97.5% / 99%).
- Why it's everywhere: the **Central Limit Theorem** — sums and means of many independent, finite-variance variables tend to normal. Normals are also **closed under addition**: independent $X\sim N(\mu_1,\sigma_1^2)$ and $Y\sim N(\mu_2,\sigma_2^2)$ give $X+Y\sim N(\mu_1+\mu_2,\ \sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2)$ (means and variances add).
- Limitation: thin tails understate extreme moves — real financial returns are fatter-tailed.

## Lognormal
- $X$ is lognormal when $\ln X$ is normal — so $X$ is strictly positive and right-skewed.
- Mean $= e^{\mu+\sigma^2/2}$, median $= e^{\mu}$ (mean **>** median); note $\mu,\sigma$ describe the *log*, not $X$.
- Models strictly-positive, **multiplicatively-driven** quantities: asset prices (geometric Brownian motion, Black–Scholes), and also incomes, biological/particle sizes, time-to-failure and insurance claim severities.

## Poisson
- Counts of independent events in a fixed window (no two at the same instant); a single rate $\lambda$ with mean $=$ variance $=\lambda$ (**equidispersion**, so SD $=\sqrt{\lambda}$). Mass function $P(X=k)=\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}/k!$, so $P(0)=e^{-\lambda}$.
- Models event frequency: defaults, claims, arrivals. The waiting time between events is **exponential**. It also arises as the **binomial limit** (law of rare events): $\text{binomial}(n,p)\to\text{Poisson}(\lambda=np)$ as $n\to\infty,\ p\to0$.
- Limitation: when variance exceeds the mean (overdispersion), reach for the negative binomial.

## Student's t
- Bell-shaped but **fat-tailed**; one shape parameter, the degrees of freedom $\nu$. Smaller $\nu$ ⇒ fatter tails; as $\nu\to\infty$ it becomes normal.
- Built as $t = Z/\sqrt{V/\nu}$ with $Z\sim N(0,1)$ and independent $V\sim\chi^2_\nu$ — replacing the unknown $\sigma$ by a sample (chi-squared) estimate is what fattens the tails.
- Used for small-sample inference: the one-sample t-test uses $\nu=n-1$ degrees of freedom; also for modelling fat-tailed returns.
- Variance $=\nu/(\nu-2)$, and is infinite/undefined for $\nu\le 2$.

## The thread
The normal is the convenient baseline; each of the others fixes a specific way it fails — positivity (lognormal), discrete counts (Poisson), fat tails (Student's t). In risk the recurring lesson is that **Gaussian tails are too thin**.
